content.text: The API surface of an agent service determines how clients interact with it — synchronously or asynchronously, with or without streaming, and what they can observe while the agent runs. Good agent API design makes the latency, cost, and capability trade-offs explicit, is versioned for safe evolution, and provides the client with enough information to render progress and handle errors.

attrs.alt: Agent API communication patterns: synchronous, streaming SSE, and async job with webhook

content.text: Client must poll or receive webhook
A well-designed agent endpoint accepts a small, consistent request body. Avoid exposing every LLM parameter — instead provide a task-level abstraction that your backend maps to model parameters.

# FastAPI Pydantic schema for the chat endpoint
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional, Literal

class ChatRequest(BaseModel):
    message: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=50_000)
    session_id: Optional[str] = Field(None, description="Opaque session token from a previous response")
    model: Literal["standard", "fast", "advanced"] = "standard"
    # Client-facing aliases; mapped server-side to claude-haiku/sonnet/opus
    stream: bool = True
    tools: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Named tools from the server's registry")
    max_tokens: int = Field(1024, ge=1, le=8096)
    idempotency_key: Optional[str] = None  # Dedup repeated requests

MODEL_MAP = {
    "fast": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    "standard": "claude-sonnet-5",
    "advanced": "claude-opus-4-8",
}

# Prevent clients from passing arbitrary model IDs
def resolve_model(alias: str) -> str:
    return MODEL_MAP[alias]
Streaming responses should use a typed event protocol so clients can distinguish text from tool calls, errors, and the final usage summary. Each SSE event is a JSON object with a

import json, asyncio
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

TOOL_REGISTRY = {
    "search": lambda q: f"Search results for: {q}",
    "calculator": lambda expr: str(eval(expr)),  # Never do this in production
}

async def agent_event_stream(message: str, model: str, tool_names: list[str]):
    tools = [
        {
            "name": name,
            "description": f"Calls the {name} tool.",
            "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["query"]},
        }
        for name in tool_names if name in TOOL_REGISTRY
    ]
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": message}]
    total_in, total_out = 0, 0

    while True:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=model, max_tokens=1024, tools=tools, messages=messages
        )
        total_in += response.usage.input_tokens
        total_out += response.usage.output_tokens

        for block in response.content:
            if block.type == "text":
                yield f"data: {json.dumps({'type': 'text', 'text': block.text})}\n\n"
                await asyncio.sleep(0)

        if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            yield f"data: {json.dumps({'type': 'done', 'usage': {'input': total_in, 'output': total_out}})}\n\n"
            return

        if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
            tool_results = []
            for block in response.content:
                if block.type == "tool_use":
                    yield f"data: {json.dumps({'type': 'tool_call', 'id': block.id, 'name': block.name, 'input': block.input})}\n\n"
                    await asyncio.sleep(0)
                    tool_fn = TOOL_REGISTRY.get(block.name)
                    result = tool_fn(**block.input) if tool_fn else "unknown tool"
                    yield f"data: {json.dumps({'type': 'tool_result', 'id': block.id, 'result': result})}\n\n"
                    tool_results.append({"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": block.id, "content": result})
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})
        else:
            yield f"data: {json.dumps({'type': 'error', 'reason': response.stop_reason})}\n\n"
            return

Agent APIs evolve: new tools, changed response shapes, updated models. Version from the start so you can change internals without breaking existing clients. The two practical approaches are URL path versioning and header versioning.

from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter, Header
from typing import Optional

# URL path versioning: /v1/agent/chat, /v2/agent/chat
app = FastAPI()

v1 = APIRouter(prefix="/v1")
v2 = APIRouter(prefix="/v2")

@v1.post("/agent/chat")
async def chat_v1(req: dict) -> dict:
    # V1: returns full response in one JSON body
    return {"reply": "...", "tokens": 100}

@v2.post("/agent/chat")
async def chat_v2(req: dict):
    # V2: streams SSE events; richer event types
    from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
    async def stream():
        yield 'data: {"type":"text","text":"hello"}\n\n'
        yield 'data: {"type":"done","usage":{"input":10,"output":5}}\n\n'
    return StreamingResponse(stream(), media_type="text/event-stream")

app.include_router(v1)
app.include_router(v2)

# Alternative: header-based versioning
@app.post("/agent/chat")
async def chat_versioned(req: dict, x_api_version: Optional[str] = Header("2025-01")) -> dict:
    if x_api_version == "2024-06":
        return {"reply": "..."}  # Legacy shape
    return {"type": "done", "output": "..."}  # Current shape

Errors from an agent API span several categories — input validation, auth, rate limit, LLM provider error, tool failure, timeout. A consistent error envelope lets clients handle each category programmatically.

Network retries can cause a client to submit the same request twice, leading to duplicate agent runs, double-spends of tokens, or duplicate side effects (emails sent twice, tickets created twice). An idempotency key in the request header lets the server return the cached result for any retry.
FastAPI generates OpenAPI docs automatically from type hints. Augment the generated schema with
to produce API docs that clients can use to understand the agent's contract without reading the source.

from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal

app = FastAPI(title="Agent API", version="2.0.0")

class ChatRequest(BaseModel):
    message: str
    session_id: str | None = None
    model: Literal["fast", "standard", "advanced"] = "standard"
    stream: bool = True

    model_config = {"json_schema_extra": {"example": {
        "message": "Summarise our Q3 metrics",
        "session_id": "sess_abc123",
        "model": "standard",
        "stream": False,
    }}}

class ChatResponse(BaseModel):
    session_id: str
    reply: str
    usage: dict

@app.post(
    "/v2/agent/chat",
    response_model=ChatResponse,
    summary="Send a message to the agent",
    description=(
        "Runs the agent loop with the provided message. "
        "Pass `stream=true` to receive Server-Sent Events instead of a single JSON body. "
        "Include the returned `session_id` in subsequent requests to continue the conversation."
    ),
    tags=["Agent"],
)
async def chat(req: ChatRequest) -> ChatResponse:
    return ChatResponse(session_id="sess_abc123", reply="...", usage={"input": 30, "output": 60})

For chat applications where the user and agent exchange many short messages without page reloads, a WebSocket connection avoids the overhead of establishing a new HTTP connection on every turn. The server pushes events as soon as they are ready, and the client can interrupt or steer the agent mid-run.

# pip install fastapi uvicorn anthropic websockets
from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect
import json
from anthropic import Anthropic

app = FastAPI()
client = Anthropic()

@app.websocket("/ws/agent")
async def agent_ws(ws: WebSocket):
    await ws.accept()
    messages = []

    try:
        while True:
            data = await ws.receive_json()
            user_msg = data.get("message", "")
            if not user_msg:
                continue

            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_msg})

            # Stream token by token using the async streaming API
            # (Anthropic does not currently stream over WS natively,
            #  so we stream from the SDK and forward events)
            with client.messages.stream(
                model="claude-opus-4-8",
                max_tokens=512,
                messages=messages,
            ) as stream:
                for text_chunk in stream.text_stream:
                    await ws.send_json({"type": "text", "chunk": text_chunk})

            full_response = stream.get_final_text()
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": full_response})
            await ws.send_json({"type": "done", "usage": {
                "input": stream.get_final_message().usage.input_tokens,
                "output": stream.get_final_message().usage.output_tokens,
            }})

    except WebSocketDisconnect:
        pass  # Client disconnected — clean up

Rather than hardcoding tool definitions inside each endpoint, a tool registry maps string names to (schema, handler) pairs. Clients send a list of tool names; the server looks them up and passes only the requested tools to the LLM. This makes it easy to add tools without changing the API contract.

Some agent responses are long — a generated report, a batch of summaries, a list of extracted records. Rather than returning all content in one response body (which can hit HTTP body limits or overwhelm mobile clients), paginate the output and let the client fetch pages lazily.

from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel
import uuid

app = FastAPI()

# Simulated store of completed agent job results
JOB_RESULTS: dict[str, list[str]] = {}

class PaginatedResponse(BaseModel):
    job_id: str
    page: int
    page_size: int
    total_items: int
    has_next: bool
    items: list[str]

@app.get("/agent/jobs/{job_id}/results", response_model=PaginatedResponse)
async def get_results(
    job_id: str,
    page: int = Query(1, ge=1),
    page_size: int = Query(20, ge=1, le=100),
) -> PaginatedResponse:
    all_items = JOB_RESULTS.get(job_id, [])
    start = (page - 1) * page_size
    end = start + page_size
    page_items = all_items[start:end]

    return PaginatedResponse(
        job_id=job_id,
        page=page,
        page_size=page_size,
        total_items=len(all_items),
        has_next=end < len(all_items),
        items=page_items,
    )

Long-running agent jobs should be cancellable — the user changed their mind, the task is no longer relevant, or it's consuming too many tokens. Implement cancellation as a separate endpoint that sets a flag; the agent loop checks the flag between tool calls and exits gracefully.

Agent endpoints are harder to unit-test than plain REST endpoints because their behavior depends on LLM responses. The standard approach is to mock the Anthropic client and return canned responses, so tests are fast and deterministic.

# pip install pytest pytest-asyncio httpx
import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from fastapi import FastAPI

# Minimal app under test
app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/agent/chat")
async def chat(req: dict) -> dict:
    from anthropic import Anthropic
    client = Anthropic()
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": req["message"]}],
    )
    return {"reply": resp.content[0].text, "tokens": resp.usage.output_tokens}

# Mock the Anthropic client
def make_mock_response(text: str, input_tokens=10, output_tokens=20):
    block = MagicMock()
    block.type = "text"
    block.text = text
    resp = MagicMock()
    resp.content = [block]
    resp.stop_reason = "end_turn"
    resp.usage.input_tokens = input_tokens
    resp.usage.output_tokens = output_tokens
    return resp

@pytest.fixture
def client():
    return TestClient(app)

def test_chat_returns_reply(client):
    mock_resp = make_mock_response("Paris is the capital of France.")
    with patch("anthropic.Anthropic.messages") as mock_msgs:
        mock_msgs.create.return_value = mock_resp
        resp = client.post("/agent/chat", json={"message": "What is the capital of France?"})
    assert resp.status_code == 200
    assert "Paris" in resp.json()["reply"]

def test_chat_missing_message(client):
    resp = client.post("/agent/chat", json={})
    # If Pydantic validates "message" is required, returns 422
    assert resp.status_code in (400, 422)

Agent API design starts by choosing a communication pattern: synchronous for short tasks, streaming SSE for real-time UX, and async jobs for long-running work. Request bodies should use client-facing aliases for models and tools — not raw model IDs. Streaming responses emit typed JSON events (

) so clients can render progress. Version the API from day one; use a consistent error envelope with machine-readable codes; add idempotency key support to make network retries safe. FastAPI's OpenAPI generation makes all of this self-documenting.
